title: Sign Text Layout
subtitle: Vertical writing, horizontal writing, lanes, centering, and reading levels
summary: Canonical statement of sign text layout for vertical and horizontal writing, including lanes, centering strategy, and SW1-SW4 reading-level defaults.
synopsis: Sign text layout extends the signbox into passages. This entry explains vertical and horizontal writing, lane counts and lane meaning, the 500x500 sign center, centering behavior, punctuation placement, and SW1-SW4 layout defaults.

slides.title: Layout Extends the Signbox
slides.summary: Passages need rules for writing direction, lanes, centering, and text-level spacing.
slides.speech: The signbox preserves the spatial shape of one sign. Sign text layout explains how signs become passages, with vertical and horizontal writing, lane behavior, centering strategy, and reading-level defaults. This is the bridge between the formal sign model and visible text flow.
slides.notes: Use this as the stable technical reference for passage-level layout behavior.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical passage-layout slide showing a SignWriting signbox becoming vertical and horizontal sign text with direction, lanes, centering, and reading-level defaults
image_prompts.abstract: A single signbox expanding into readable passage flow

slides.title: The 500x500 Center
slides.summary: A shared center gives layout tools a stable reference point for aligning signs in passages.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical diagram of a SignWriting signbox centered at 500x500, with alignment lines extending into vertical and horizontal passage layout
image_prompts.abstract: A stable center point anchoring sign text across layout systems

bullets: Rendering systems share a reference point
slides.speech: The common center is the bridge from individual signboxes to passage layout. Signs have a center at 500x500, and that reference point supports lane placement, text alignment, and consistent layout across rendering systems.
slides.notes: This slide should make the numeric center feel practical, not arbitrary.

slides.title: Directions And Lanes
slides.summary: Vertical writing uses left, middle, and right lanes; horizontal writing uses top and bottom lanes without changing the underlying sign text.

image_prompts.concrete: Layout comparison slide showing vertical SignWriting with left middle right lanes and horizontal SignWriting with top bottom lanes, using clean technical labels
image_prompts.abstract: Two passage directions organizing the same signed text through different lane systems

bullets: The signbox itself is not rewritten
slides.speech: Writing direction is a layout choice applied to the same underlying sign text. Vertical writing uses left, middle, and right lanes. Horizontal writing uses top and bottom lanes. The lane belongs to passage layout; it does not rewrite the signbox itself.
slides.notes: This is the core reader-facing distinction for implementers.

slides.title: Centering And Reading Levels
slides.summary: Hybrid centering rules and SW1-SW4 profiles provide shared implementation defaults, not empirical reading-level claims.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical table-and-diagram slide showing SignWriting centering rules with SW1 SW2 SW3 SW4 layout profiles as implementation defaults
image_prompts.abstract: Centering rules and reading profiles forming a shared layout baseline

bullets: Vertical centers on head and trunk where present
Horizontal centers on the head where present
Profiles are defaults, not outcome claims
slides.speech: Centering keeps signs aligned with the passage while preserving internal composition. The SW1 to SW4 profiles then provide shared implementation defaults for length, width, offset, padding, and punctuation spacing. They are layout profiles, not empirical claims about reader difficulty.
slides.notes: Emphasize the boundary around SW1-SW4. They are practical defaults unless future research calibrates them differently.

slides.title: Layout Is Not Rendering
slides.summary: Layout defines passage structure; rendering produces visible output through SVG, fonts, print, web components, or other presentation systems.

image_prompts.concrete: Boundary slide separating sign text layout model from rendering outputs such as SVG, fonts, print layout, and web components
image_prompts.abstract: Passage structure preserved before it becomes visible presentation

bullets: The same layout can use multiple renderers
slides.speech: Layout and rendering are related, but they are not the same layer. Layout defines passage structure: writing direction, lanes, centering, and metrics. Rendering turns that structure into visible output through SVG, fonts, print layout, web components, or other systems.
slides.notes: Close by connecting layout to Rendering Formal SignWriting and practice implementations.
